articles/free_love.html.title: Free Love | CF.Bonsai
articles/free_love.html.keywords: Free Love Unstucking Braitenberg's wheels Braitenberg’s original “vehicles” were creatures of passion — turning toward light, chasing shadows, falling in love with stimuli. But their devotion often led to a problem: they got stuck. We depart from the first three creatures. The others are exercises of possibilities, not unlike the LBP. In Free Love, those early impulses are set loose. Here, the vehicles learn to move on — to adapt, turn away, and recover — transforming simple attraction into sustained, purposeful behavior.

articles/install_bonsai.html.title: | CF.Bonsai
articles/install_bonsai.html.keywords: Welcome Welcome to CF.Bonsai, a website from the Champalimaud Foundation that contains annotated examples of recurring neuroscience tasks programmed in Bonsai. Many of these examples make use of the Harp hardware protocol to interface with a variety of peripheral devices. Getting started Start by installing the necessary Bonsai and Harp software from the Getting Started menu on the left panel. Then, find your example of choice, and follow the instructions to run it. Every example can be copied directly to Bonsai, and includes a set of annotations that explain the most significant parts of the code. If the example involves hardware (as those of Harp) additional electrical diagrams are provided with the necessary connections between the components. Contributions You can place suggestions for new examples, or report any issues concerning the existing examples here. If you want to contribute with your own examples please follow the guidelines described in the cf.bonsai repository. For issues concerning Bonsai, please use the official channels. Bonsai Bonsai is an open-source visual language for reactive programming. It is lightweight and easy to use with a variety of packages and modules for interfacing with hardware and for real-time processing and manipulation of data streams. More information about Harp can be found at bonsai-rx.org. Harp Harp is an open-source standard for asynchronous real-time data acquisition and experimental control in neuroscience. The Harp ecosystem currently includes devices to configure, control and collect data from a wide range of peripheral devices such as cameras, LEDs, nosepokes and motors. More information about Harp can be found at harp-tech.org.

articles/meet_minimu.html.title: Meet MinimU | CF.Bonsai
articles/meet_minimu.html.keywords: Meet MinimU MinimU is the Little Brain Project’s embodied agent — a small, wheeled, wireless robot that brings minimalist neural architectures to life. The robot includes onboard sensors for IMU data, wheel rotation, voltage, and battery level, and can be expanded with additional wireless modules. Communication and Control Computer communication: wireless interface for data exchange and control Bonsai integration: programming through the Bonsai visual reactive framework, based on ReactiveX, enabling real-time sensorimotor processing Sensors: IMU, encoders, voltage, and current feedback Actuation: differential wheel drive with feedback control Although not constrained by it, MinimU is typically programmed in Bonsai, allowing behavior to emerge through continuous feedback between sensing and actuation. Through MinimU, we study how simple circuits, embodied in motion and perception, can give rise to the first glimmers of adaptive and integrated behavior.

articles/welcome.html.title: Little Brain Project (LPB) | CF.Bonsai
articles/welcome.html.keywords: Little Brain Project (LPB) Exploring the evolutionary roots of intelligence Overview The Little Brain Project (LPB) explores how neural circuits give rise to intelligent behavior, using neural evolution as a guiding framework. It seeks to understand not only how brain mechanisms are implemented, but also why their organization emerged in the light of evolution. Beyond retracing what may have happened, LPB speculates on how it could have unfolded — not to imagine different outcomes, but to better understand the one that led to us. By exploring possible intermediate steps and mechanisms, the project aims to illuminate how evolution might have assembled the neural architectures that make intelligence possible. Rooted in the tradition of cybernetics, LPB seeks to understand brains by building them, giving them physical expression through a real, embodied robot. Through minimalist agents, LPB explores how intelligence arises from the continuous dialogue between nervous system, body, and environment. This site serves as a working record of the project — a place where progress is marked by functional or conceptual increments rather than dates. Each entry reflects the development of the robot and the reasoning behind specific implementation choices. Unlike evolution, time, or an agenda, this site can — and will — be rewritten to provide an account of understanding as it unfolds. From Minimal Creatures to Complex Minds We begin with minimal, reactive agents — simple sensor-to-motor architectures that already exhibit lifelike, purposeful behavior. Heavily inspired by Valentino Braitenberg’s Vehicles: Experiments in Synthetic Psychology, these foundational systems are implemented in real robots, allowing us to observe and analyze how behavior emerges directly from neural organization and embodiment. From these starting points, LPB investigates how increasingly sophisticated neural mechanisms can support: Navigation and spatial orientation Reflexes and behavioral coordination Learning and adaptation Internal representations and models While much of the work focuses on the neural implementation of individual functions, the ultimate goal of the project is to understand how these mechanisms integrate — how perception, action, and memory come together to form a unified, coherent creature. Here lies a major strength of the project: each task is never tackled in isolation. It is always integrated with existing functions — guided by evidence when possible, or by reasoned coherence when necessary. What makes a creature a creature? Inspiration from Nature The project draws strong inspiration from insect neuroscience, where compact yet capable brains solve complex problems through elegant neural architectures. By connecting insights from insect research with embodied computational models, LPB seeks to uncover the general design principles underlying adaptive intelligence in both biological and artificial systems. Why “Little Brain”? Small does not mean simple. Even the tiniest nervous systems demonstrate powerful organizational principles that underpin cognition and control. In this bottom-up approach, the project complements other research areas that explore the dynamics of large neural networks. The Little Brain Project celebrates these natural designs — and seeks to reveal how intelligent behavior evolved from the simplest neural beginnings. Meet MinimU MinimU is the Little Brain Project’s embodied agent — a small, wheeled, wireless robot that brings minimalist neural architectures to life. The robot includes onboard sensors for IMU data, wheel rotation, voltage, and battery level, and can be expanded with additional wireless modules. Communication and Control Computer communication: wireless interface for data exchange and control Bonsai integration: programming through the Bonsai visual reactive framework, based on ReactiveX, enabling real-time sensorimotor processing Sensors: IMU, encoders, voltage, and current feedback Actuation: differential wheel drive with feedback control Although not constrained by it, MinimU is typically programmed in Bonsai, allowing behavior to emerge through continuous feedback between sensing and actuation. Through MinimU, we study how simple circuits, embodied in motion and perception, can give rise to the first glimmers of adaptive and integrated behavior.

docfx-tools/README.html.title: docfx-tools | CF.Bonsai
docfx-tools/README.html.keywords: docfx-tools A docfx template for package documentation, patching the modern template to provide stylesheets and scripts for rendering custom workflow containers with copy functionality. How to use To include this template in a docfx website, first clone this repository as a submodule: git submodule add https://github.com/bonsai-rx/docfx-tools bonsai Then modify docfx.json to include the template immediately after the modern template: "template": [ "default", "modern", "bonsai/template", "template" ], Finally, import and call the modules inside your website template/public folder. main.css @import "workflow.css"; main.js import WorkflowContainer from "./workflow.js" export default { start: () => { WorkflowContainer.init(); } } Powershell Scripts This repository also provides helper scripts to automate several content generation steps for package documentation websites. Exporting workflow images Exporting SVG images for all example workflows can be automated by placing all .bonsai files in a workflows folder and calling the below script pointing to the bin directory to include. A bonsai environment is assumed to be available in the .bonsai folder in the repository root. .\modules\Export-Image.ps1 "..\src\PackageName\bin\Release\net472"
